message.content:
Adversarial cryptography reviewer: REFUTE this candidate; default refuted=true unless solid. Read the real cb-mpc code at C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc first.

CANDIDATE: Batch PVE decrypt: attacker-controlled inner Q vector length is not re-checked against batch_count, causing out-of-bounds std::vector read of ecc_point_t (wild EC_POINT* deref) on the honest decryptor
api: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(curve_id, dk, ek, ciphertext, label, out_xs) in include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h
class: Out-of-bounds read / missing length validation on deserialized container severity: Medium
file: src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_batch.cpp:183
missing_check: After deserializing ec_pve_batch_t from the untrusted blob, nothing verifies that the inner length-prefixed Q vector (read by ec_pve_batch_t::convert -> converter.convert(Q,...)) has size == n. ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61) checks `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` BEFORE reading, when Q is still the constructor-sized vector, so the check is a no-op; the subsequent std::vector overload does Q.clear()/resize(attacker_count), making Q.size() fully attacker-controlled. verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78) does enforce Q.size()==n, but the decrypt path runs with skip_verify=true and never re-validates. restore_from_decrypted() validates row_index but not Q.size().
data_flow: decrypt_batch -> parse_batch_ciphertext sets n = blob.batch_count (separate outer field, bounded by MAX_BATCH_COUNT) -> ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n) (constructor resizes Q to n) -> coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) -> ec_pve_batch_t::convert: pre-check Q.size()==n passes (still n), then converter.convert(Q,...) clears and resizes Q to the attacker's inner length prefix m (m can be < n) -> get_Qs() curve loop iterates only the m present entries (no rejection) -> pve_ct.decrypt(..., skip_verify=true) -> for each kappa row, base_pke.decrypt(dk, inner_label, rows[i].c, x_buf): attacker can craft rows[i].c as a valid base-PKE ciphertext under the public ek so this returns SUCCESS for some row -> restore_from_decrypted(i, x_buf, curve, xs): builds x0,x1 of size n via bn_t::vector_from_bin, then loop `for (int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) ... }` reads Q[i] for i up to n-1 while Q.size()==m<n -> OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> element whose embedded EC_POINT* pointer is uninitialized/out-of-bounds heap memory, then dereferenced inside ecc_point_t::operator!= (OpenSSL EC_POINT_cmp).
attacker_role: Malicious party that produces the batch-PVE ciphertext blob consumed by an honest decryptor via the public decrypt_batch API.
impact: A malicious counterparty who supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext can force the honest party's decrypt_batch (or get_public_keys_compressed_batch / decrypt_batch_*_hsm, which share the same construct+deserialize pattern) to read past the end of the Q vector and dereference a wild EC_POINT* in OpenSSL. At minimum this is a remotely triggerable crash (DoS) of the honest decryptor; because the wild pointer is consumed as an OpenSSL EC_POINT object, the concrete impact ranges from segfault to controlled OOB read depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is leaked, but it is reachable purely through the public API boundary with valid base-PKE rows.
excerpt: // pve_batch.h convert(): pre-check is a no-op, then convert(Q,...) resizes Q to attacker count
if (int(Q.size()) != n) { converter.set_error(); return; }
converter.convert(Q, L, b);
// pve_batch.cpp restore_from_decrypted: loops to n, indexes attacker-sized Q
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { MODULO(q) x[i] = x0[i] + x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i] * G) return coinbase::error(E_CRYPTO); }

LENS = CONCRETE BOUNDARY ATTACK. Construct the exact malicious-party deviation through the public API and the concrete impact (which secret/forgery/DoS). Honest party runs unmodified code. If no plausible attack, refuted=true.

is_real=true only if it survives against the real code. Return ONLY the structured object.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll investigate the real code to verify this candidate. Let me start by reading the key files.
The candidate's core claim is that `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` clears and resizes Q to an attacker-controlled length. Let me examine the converter's behavior for `std::vector<ecc_point_t>`.

input.command: grep -rn "convert" C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc/include/cbmpc/core/convert.h 2>/dev/null | head -80; echo "---FILES---"; find C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc -name "convert*.h" -o -name "convert*.cpp" 2>/dev/null | grep -i convert

content.text:
The candidate's mechanism is confirmed at the deserialization level: on read, `value.clear()` then `value.resize(count)` where count is attacker-controlled (bounded only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS). So after `convert(pve_ct, blob.ct)`, `Q.size()` can be `m != n`.

Now the critical question: is the no-op precheck the real story, and is decrypt actually reachable with skip_verify=true? Let me check the public API and parse_batch_ciphertext.

content.content:
1	#pragma once
2	
3	#include <vector>
4	
5	#include <cbmpc/api/curve.h>
6	#include <cbmpc/api/pve_base_pke.h>
7	#include <cbmpc/core/buf.h>
8	#include <cbmpc/core/error.h>
9	
10	namespace coinbase::api::pve {
11	
12	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
13	// Batch PVE (1P) API
14	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
15	//
16	// This API batches the core PVE algorithm for multiple scalars {x_i} in one
17	// ciphertext, sharing the same label and base PKE key.
18	//
19	// Notes:
20	// - `xs[i]` is interpreted as a big-endian integer and reduced modulo the curve order.
21	// - Defensive limit: each `xs[i].size` must be <= the curve order size in bytes.
22	// - `Qs_compressed[i]` is the compressed point encoding for the outer curve:
23	//   33 bytes for P-256/secp256k1, 32 bytes for Ed25519.
24	
25	error_t encrypt_batch(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, const std::vector<mem_t>& xs,
26	                      buf_t& out_ciphertext);
27	
28	// Same as `encrypt_batch(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
29	error_t encrypt_batch(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, const std::vector<mem_t>& xs, buf_t& out_ciphertext);
30	
31	error_t verify_batch(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext,
32	                     const std::vector<mem_t>& Qs_compressed, mem_t label);
33	
34	// Same as `verify_batch(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
35	error_t verify_batch(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, const std::vector<mem_t>& Qs_compressed, mem_t label);
36	
37	// Decrypt a batch ciphertext, recovering the vector of scalars {x_i}.
38	//
39	// Output:
40	// - On success, `out_xs[i]` is a fixed-length big-endian encoding of x_i with
41	//   length equal to the curve order size in bytes (e.g., 32 bytes for P-256).
42	//
43	// Notes:
44	// - This function intentionally does not verify `ciphertext` before decryption.
45	//   Invalid ciphertexts may cause decryption to fail, but are designed to not
46	//   leak secret information.
47	// - If you need ciphertext validation, call `verify_batch(...)` first.
48	error_t decrypt_batch(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
49	                      std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
50	
51	// Same as `decrypt_batch(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
52	error_t decrypt_batch(curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label, std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
53	
54	// Decrypt a batch ciphertext using an HSM-backed RSA private key.
55	error_t decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
56	                                   const rsa_oaep_hsm_decap_cb_t& cb, std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
57	
58	// Decrypt a batch ciphertext using an HSM-backed ECIES(P-256) private key.
59	error_t decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
60	                                     const ecies_p256_hsm_ecdh_cb_t& cb, std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
61	
62	// Extract batch count from a batch ciphertext.
63	error_t get_batch_count(mem_t ciphertext, int& out_batch_count);
64	
65	// Extract the public values {Q_i} whose discrete logs are encrypted as the ciphertext, returning compressed point
66	// encodings.
67	error_t get_public_keys_compressed_batch(mem_t ciphertext, std::vector<buf_t>& out_Qs_compressed);
68	
69	// Extract the associated label from a batch ciphertext.
70	error_t get_Label_batch(mem_t ciphertext, buf_t& out_label);
71	
72	}  // namespace coinbase::api::pve
73

content.text: The API docstring confirms decrypt_batch intentionally doesn't verify. Now let me look at the implementation - the .cpp for the API to see how the blob is parsed, whether `n` comes from a separate outer field, and crucially how the deserialized `ec_pve_batch_t` is constructed and used.

This is the crux. Let me trace carefully.

In `decrypt_batch`: `n = blob.batch_count`, then `ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n)` (constructor resizes Q to n), then `coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct)` calls `ec_pve_batch_t::convert`.

In `convert()`: the precheck `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` — at this point Q.size()==n (from constructor), so passes. Then `converter.convert(Q, ...)` deserializes Q from the blob, which calls the std::vector overload: `Q.clear()` then `Q.resize(count)` where count is the attacker's inner length prefix `m`. So now `Q.size() == m`, while `pve_ct.n == n`.

But wait — there is a guard right after convert in decrypt_batch (lines 165-167):
```cpp
for (const auto& q : pve_ct.get_Qs()) {
    if (q.get_curve() != icurve) return ...;
}
```
This iterates `m` entries (the actual Q vector size), not `n`. So it does NOT catch the size mismatch. If `m < n`, the loop iterates only m valid entries.

Now `pve_ct.decrypt(..., skip_verify=true)`. Let me confirm the OOB. In `decrypt`, for each kappa row it calls `base_pke.decrypt`; on success calls `restore_from_decrypted` which loops `for (i=0; i<n; i++) { ... if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) }`. Here `n` is the member (the constructor value), but `Q.size()==m<n`. So `Q[i]` for `i>=m` is OOB read of `std::vector<ecc_point_t>`.

Let me verify the key concern: is the precheck genuinely a no-op? Let me check `coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct)` wrapper, and verify the `n` used in restore loop is the member n not Q.size().

The member `n` is set in the constructor and never modified by convert (convert only touches Q, L, b, rows). Confirmed. So restore_from_decrypted loops to `n` but Q is size `m`.

Now I must check whether `ecc_point_t` default elements created by `resize(m)` — actually the OOB elements aren't even created. `Q[i]` for `i>=m` reads beyond `vector::size()` (but possibly within capacity, or beyond). This is genuine UB / OOB read. Then `Q[i] != x[i]*G` dereferences. Let me check ecc_point_t to confirm it holds a pointer.

ecc_point_t holds `EC_POINT* ptr` and a curve pointer. OOB read gives garbage pointer, dereferenced in operator!=. 

Now the critical reachability question I must REFUTE or confirm: Can the attacker actually reach `restore_from_decrypted` with a craftable success? The decrypt loop calls `base_pke.decrypt(dk, ...)` — this uses the *honest decryptor's* private key dk. The attacker doesn't know dk. But `restore_from_decrypted` is only reached if `base_pke.decrypt` returns SUCCESS.

However — there's a subtler path. Even if base_pke.decrypt never succeeds, the loop just continues and returns E_CRYPTO; no OOB. So OOB requires a row where decrypt succeeds. But more importantly: does the OOB even require decrypt success? Let me re-read. Yes, OOB is inside restore_from_decrypted, only called after a successful base_pke.decrypt.

But there's an even more direct issue I should check. Actually wait — let me reconsider the convert precheck more carefully. The candidate says the precheck is checked when Q is "still the constructor-sized vector." Let me verify whether convert even reaches the resize, because if `m > n` triggers something. Actually the real defensive question: after convert, both decrypt_batch and get_public_keys iterate `get_Qs()` which is size m. For get_public_keys_compressed_batch and get_Label_batch there is no decrypt and no use of n beyond — they only iterate m entries. So those are safe. The candidate over-claims those.

For decrypt_batch: the OOB requires `base_pke.decrypt` to succeed on a row. Let me examine base_pke decrypt to see if an attacker can craft `rows[i].c` that decrypts successfully without knowing dk.

The base PKE is RSA-OAEP or ECIES — semantically secure encryption under the recipient's public key `ek`. The attacker DOES know `ek` (it is public — it's the encryption key passed into decrypt_batch, and in the standard PVE model the attacker is the encryptor who created the ciphertext using ek). So the attacker can absolutely produce valid base-PKE ciphertexts that decrypt successfully under dk — they just encrypt arbitrary content under the public ek. So `rows[i].c` can be crafted to be a valid ciphertext, and `base_pke.decrypt` will return SUCCESS.

So the attack is reachable: attacker sets `m < n` for the inner Q vector, and provides at least one row `c` that is a valid base-PKE encryption under ek. `restore_from_decrypted` runs, the inner length checks (x_bin.size, r.size) can be satisfied, then the loop reads `Q[i]` OOB for `i >= m`.

Let me double check the inner length checks in restore_from_decrypted don't depend on Q.size, and that bn_t::vector_from_bin produces x0/x1 of size n (so x[i] valid up to n). Lines 174,178: `vector_from_bin(..., n, ...)` → x0,x1 sized n. x is `xs` resized to n in decrypt (line 192). So x[i] valid. Only Q[i] is OOB. Confirmed.

One more: is the precheck truly a no-op, or could a defensive reader argue convert's resize of Q to m, combined with subsequent rows convert, somehow set an error if m mismatches? No — rows is a separate fixed-size (kappa) vector, independent of Q. The convert succeeds with any m ≤ MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS. So `coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct)` returns SUCCESS with Q.size()==m.

This is a genuine, reachable, missing-length-validation OOB read on the honest decryptor through the public API boundary. The candidate's mechanism is accurate. Severity Medium (DoS / OOB read; no secret leak) is fair.

Let me confirm `coinbase::convert(obj, mem)` returns error properly and doesn't independently re-check, and that there's no length check I missed between convert and decrypt.

The decrypt_batch code (lines 162-167) is the only post-convert check, and it iterates `get_Qs()` (size m), so it can't catch m<n. No re-check of `Q.size()==n` exists on the decrypt path. Confirmed missing check.

The candidate survives scrutiny. The attack is real and reachable through the public boundary.

input.attack_at_boundary: decrypt_batch (public API). Attacker is the malicious party that produced the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext blob consumed by an honest decryptor.
input.reason: Verified against real code. The serialization deserializer (convert.h:115-131) does value.clear()/value.resize(count) with attacker-controlled count on read, so after coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) the inner Q vector size m is fully attacker-controlled. The convert() precheck `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` (pve_batch.h:48-52) runs BEFORE converter.convert(Q,...) while Q is still constructor-sized (==n), so it is a genuine no-op against the deserialized length — confirmed. The decrypt path (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:159-174) sets n=blob.batch_count (outer field, bounded by MAX_BATCH_COUNT), constructs ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n) (member n fixed, Q resized to n), deserializes (Q now size m), then the only post-convert guard (lines 165-167) iterates get_Qs() which is size m — so it CANNOT catch m<n. decrypt() is then called with skip_verify=true (verify() which enforces Q.size()==n is bypassed; this is intentional per the API docstring and never re-validated). In decrypt(), each kappa row calls base_pke.decrypt(dk,...); on SUCCESS restore_from_decrypted loops `for(i=0;i<n;i++){ ...; if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) ...}` (pve_batch.cpp:181-184) indexing Q up to n-1 while Q.size()==m<n -> OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> read; ecc_point_t holds an EC_POINT* ptr (base_ecc.h:245) dereferenced in operator!=/EC_POINT_cmp -> wild-pointer deref. Reachability of the success branch is real: the base PKE (RSA-OAEP / ECIES) encrypts under the PUBLIC ek, which the malicious encryptor knows, so it can craft rows[i].c as a valid ciphertext that base_pke.decrypt returns SUCCESS for; x0/x1 are sized n via vector_from_bin so x[i] is valid, only Q[i] is OOB. Net: malicious counterparty can force an OOB read / wild EC_POINT* deref (crash/DoS, possibly controlled OOB read depending on heap) on the honest decryptor purely through the public decrypt_batch (and the HSM variants which funnel into the same function). No honest secret is leaked, so Medium is appropriate. Minor over-claim in the candidate: get_public_keys_compressed_batch and get_Label_batch only iterate get_Qs() (size m) and use no out-of-bounds n loop, so they are NOT vulnerable to this OOB; only the decrypt_batch family is. Confidence 0.8 (cannot compile/run a PoC here, and exact crash-vs-controlled-read depends on allocator/heap layout, but the missing-check and OOB index are concretely present in source).
